counties.floyd-county: Elite Catastrophe is based in Floyd County, so this is the ground we know best. The county wraps around Rome where the Oostanaula, Etowah, and Coosa rivers meet, and the mix of hillside neighborhoods, river-bottom yards, and rural acreage gives it a heavy and varied tree load. Water oaks and red oaks shade the older in-town lots, many of them taller than the houses beneath them and prone to hollowing out with age. Out in the bottoms and along the ridges, loblolly and shortleaf pines stand in red clay that loses its grip after a soaking rain. Sweetgums scatter wood in every summer blow. Because we live here, Floyd County sees our crews more than anywhere else: removals in tight yards, structural pruning, deadwooding older hardwoods, and stump grinding. When severe weather crosses the county, Elite Catastrophe answers storm calls 24/7. Most days, though, the work is calmer and planned well ahead.
counties.polk-county: South and east of Rome, Polk County trades river bottoms for rolling farmland and the old mill towns of Cedartown and Rockmart. The canopy here is classic Northwest Georgia: red oaks and water oaks over the in-town streets, sweetgums along the fencerows, and thick stands of loblolly and shortleaf pine ringing the edges of both towns and filling the newer subdivisions. Those leftover pines are the county's most common hazard, tall and shallow-rooted in clay that softens after a long rain. Mature hardwoods carry their own risk, declining from the inside while looking full and green from the road. Elite Catastrophe works Polk County every week from our Rome base, handling removals, crown reduction, clearance pruning, and stump grinding. Anyone who has walked the Silver Comet Trail knows how much of the county's character rides on its trees. When storms tear through, our crews run 24/7 until the calls stop.
counties.gordon-county: Gordon County sits along the I-75 corridor north of Rome, built around Calhoun and the smaller river towns like Resaca. The Oostanaula and its feeder creeks run wooded bottoms through the county, and those damp, low-lying stands are where clay stays soft the longest. On the higher ground, established neighborhoods hold mature water oaks and red oaks with limbs reaching over rooftops and drives, while the fast-growing corridor near the interstate keeps leaving tall loblolly pines exposed after the graders move on. Both patterns fail in the ways we see everywhere in Northwest Georgia: hardwoods hollowing out unseen, pines uprooting in saturated ground, sweetgums dropping wood in a stiff wind. Elite Catastrophe covers Gordon County on a regular schedule from Rome, bringing the same crew and equipment we use at home for removals, pruning, hazard checks, and stump grinding. When severe weather crosses the county, our storm crews respond 24/7.
counties.chattooga-county: North of Rome the land climbs toward the Lookout Mountain foothills, and Chattooga County is where our trees get truly wind-tested. Summerville and Trion sit down in the valley, where storms funnel along the ridgelines and gusts pour over the shoulders of the slopes. Trees here anchor in thinner soil, lean harder toward the light, and lose crowns in a single rough afternoon. The species are the familiar ones: loblolly and shortleaf pine that snap or uproot in wet clay, water oaks that hollow with age, red oaks carrying heavy horizontal limbs, and sweetgums that shed wood in every blow. What the foothills add is exposure, which turns an ordinary leaner into a real hazard. Elite Catastrophe makes the drive up from Rome often, because Chattooga County homeowners live with some of the most storm-tested trees in our whole range. When the weather turns dangerous, our crews field storm calls 24/7.
counties.bartow-county: East of Rome along I-75, Bartow County runs from the historic streets of Adairsville and Cartersville down to Lake Allatoona and the growing towns of Emerson and Euharlee. It is a county of contrasts for tree work. Old town centers keep generations-old oaks and sweetgums over close house lots, while newer subdivisions near the lake and the interstate leave tall leftover pines standing in graded, compacted clay. Mature water oaks here decline from the inside and drop heavy limbs on calm days. Construction-stressed pines look fine for a few summers, then brown out and turn brittle. Sweetgums split at weak unions and scatter wood every time the wind picks up. Elite Catastrophe covers Bartow County from our Rome base with the same family-owned crew we send through our own neighborhoods, handling removals, trimming, crown work, hazard evaluations, and stump grinding. When a storm system rakes across the county, our crews answer emergency calls 24/7.

cave-spring.narrative: Cave Spring built its whole identity around water, and the same springs and creeks that draw visitors to Rolater Park keep the ground under this town's trees damp far longer than most of Floyd County. About thirteen miles south of Rome, the town holds one of the prettiest old canopies in the county: mature hardwoods shading the historic district, big oaks leaning over the antique shops and side streets, and stands of pine filling the rural edges where the small-lot core gives way to open land. That mineral-rich, spring-fed ground is a mixed blessing for trees. It grows them tall and full, but it also stays soft, and a water oak or loblolly pine with roots sitting in wet clay along Cedar Creek can lose its footing in a hard spring blow. Elite Catastrophe is a family-owned tree service based just up the road in Rome, and Cave Spring is one of the shorter runs on our schedule. The work here follows the town's split personality. In the historic core, the job is usually preservation: crown cleaning the grand old hardwoods, reducing weight on limbs that overhang porches and tin roofs, and cutting out deadwood before it drops on a sidewalk. On the rural lots, the work shifts toward removals: the brittle pine near the bedroom wall or the storm-split hardwood along the fence. Stump grinding finishes either one, turning an old removal back into usable yard. When violent weather rolls through this corner of Floyd County, Elite Catastrophe answers storm calls 24/7. The rest of the time, the smart move in Cave Spring is a slow walk around the property with someone who cuts trees every working day.
cave-spring.treeAngle: historic-district hardwoods on damp spring-fed ground

aragon.narrative: Aragon is a small former mill town in Polk County, the kind of place where a single big tree can shade most of a modest lot. About twenty miles from Rome, it sits along the Silver Comet Trail corridor near Rockmart, and its yards carry the same tree stock you find all through this part of Georgia: mature hardwoods left over from the mill-town days, plus loblolly and shortleaf pine crowding the edges and the newer parcels. On lots this size, tree work is a close-quarters job. When a leaning pine stands between two houses or a dying sweetgum hangs over a driveway, there is no room to simply drop the wood. Limbs come off in sections, on ropes, in the right order. Elite Catastrophe is a family-owned tree service that works Aragon regularly out of our Rome base, and we know these trees because they are the same species we handle at home every week. Water oaks that hollow quietly with age. Pines rooted shallow in red clay that turns loose after a wet stretch. Sweetgums that fling limbs across a yard in a summer gust. Our everyday work here is planned and steady: removing dead or badly placed trees before they fall on their own, trimming limbs back off roofs and service drops, thinning the crowns of the big hardwoods worth keeping, and grinding stumps flush so a mower can pass. If severe weather blows through Polk County, our crews take storm calls 24/7. Most of the time, though, the best call in Aragon is the one made in fair weather, about the tree you have been eyeing since the last storm season.
aragon.treeAngle: mill-town hardwoods and pines on modest lots

resaca.narrative: Along the Oostanaula River in Gordon County, Resaca is a tiny town wrapped in wooded river-bottom land, and that low, damp ground shapes everything about its trees. The river and its floodplain keep the clay soft for weeks at a stretch, which grows big timber but also gives roots a poor grip when the spring storms arrive. About eighteen miles from Rome and split by the I-75 corridor, Resaca has more woods than houses, and many of its trees stand close to homes, barns, and the old battlefield ground that runs through the area. Elite Catastrophe, a family-owned crew, covers Resaca on a regular schedule from our Rome base, and the river-bottom setting turns up a particular kind of tree trouble here. Tall loblolly and shortleaf pines that uproot cleanly when saturated ground lets go. Water oaks along the banks that hollow from the inside while looking healthy up top. Sweetgums and red oaks with heavy limbs stretched out over roofs and outbuildings. Our work in Resaca is the everyday list handled with care: removing hazard trees before the next storm chooses for you, reducing weight in crowns that have outgrown their footing, pruning limbs back off structures, and grinding stumps so a finished removal leaves usable ground. Because so many properties here sit on acreage, we also spend time simply walking the tree line with owners, pointing out which trees near the house are sound and which are quietly declining. When severe weather crosses this stretch of Gordon County, Elite Catastrophe answers storm calls 24/7. The better path is dealing with the leaning riverside pine now, on a calm afternoon, rather than after it has already come down.
resaca.treeAngle: river-bottom pines and hollowing bank oaks

trion.narrative: Tucked in the Chattooga River valley beneath the Lookout Mountain foothills, Trion is a mill town of tight village lots where the wind is the deciding factor for trees. The valley funnels gusts along its length, and a storm that would merely rattle a yard out in flat country can strip a crown here in minutes. Roughly twenty-eight miles north of Rome, Trion packs its houses close together the way old mill villages do, so a full-grown tree often stands within reach of two or three roofs at once. That combination, tight lots plus valley wind, makes careful takedown work essential. There is rarely room to fell a tree whole, so limbs and trunk sections come down on ropes, in order, with the drop zone controlled every step. Elite Catastrophe is a family-owned crew that drives up to Trion regularly from Rome, and the trees here are the storm-tested Northwest Georgia set: loblolly and shortleaf pine that snap or pull out of wet clay, water oaks gone hollow with age, red oaks holding heavy horizontal limbs, and sweetgums that shed wood in every hard blow. Wind exposure is what pushes an ordinary lean into a genuine hazard, and a leaning tree beside a mill-village house is exactly the kind of call we would rather get early. Our Trion work runs the usual list: hazard removals, crown reduction on trees that catch too much wind, clearance pruning off rooflines, and stump grinding to reclaim the tight yard space. When the valley weather turns violent, Elite Catastrophe fields storm calls 24/7. For everything short of that, a calm-day walk-through is the surest way to settle the question before the next storm season.
trion.treeAngle: valley-wind exposure on tight mill-village lots

emerson.narrative: Growth is the defining fact of Emerson right now, and growth is exactly what changes a town's trees. This small Bartow County town sits near Lake Allatoona and the LakePoint complex along the I-75 corridor, roughly twenty-eight miles from Rome, and it has been filling in fast with new development beside its older lots. That mix is what we see in the yards. Established parcels still hold mature hardwoods, oaks and sweetgums that have shaded the same ground for decades. The newer subdivisions tell a different story: tall loblolly pines left standing after construction stripped away the trees around them, now rooted in compacted clay and suddenly exposed to wind on all sides. A pine that grew up sheltered inside a stand, then lost its neighbors when the lots around it were built, is the single tree most likely to end up flat after a storm, and Emerson has plenty of them. Elite Catastrophe is a family-owned tree service that covers Emerson from our Rome base, bringing the same crew and gear we use in our own neighborhoods. The everyday work here is steady and planned: removing dead, dying, or dangerously placed trees, thinning and cleaning the crowns of hardwoods worth keeping, cutting limbs back off new rooflines, evaluating the leftover pines honestly, and grinding stumps below grade so a new lawn can go in clean. Lakeside lots add their own wrinkle, with damp ground near the water keeping roots loose long after a rain. When storms roll off the lake and across Bartow County, Elite Catastrophe answers emergency calls 24/7. Short of that, the best time to look hard at a questionable pine is now, before the next line of weather does it for you.
emerson.treeAngle: construction-exposed pines in a fast-growing lake town

euharlee.narrative: The covered bridge over Euharlee Creek is what most people picture when they think of this Bartow County town, but the trees around it are just as much a part of the setting. Euharlee blends rural and suburban, with wooded creek bottoms, older home places under mature hardwoods, and newer neighborhoods edged by pine. About twenty-six miles from Rome, the town keeps the same tree character as the rest of Northwest Georgia: red oaks with heavy horizontal limbs, water oaks that hollow out from the inside as they age, sweetgums that drop wood in every strong wind, and loblolly pines standing shallow-rooted in red clay. The creek bottoms are where that clay stays wettest, and a pine or bankside oak in soaked ground can lose its footing when a spring storm leans on it. Elite Catastrophe is a family-owned tree service that works Euharlee on a regular schedule from our Rome base. The work sorts into two kinds. Around the historic core and the older properties, it tends toward preservation: crown cleaning, weight reduction, and deadwood removal on the big hardwoods worth keeping. In the newer neighborhoods and out on the acreage, it leans toward removals and hazard cleanup: the brittle pine near the house or the storm-damaged limb hanging over the drive. Stump grinding closes out either kind of job so the ground goes back to lawn. When severe weather crosses this part of Bartow County, Elite Catastrophe answers storm calls 24/7. Otherwise, the smartest move in Euharlee is the unhurried one: have someone who cuts trees every working day walk the property and tell you plainly which ones are sound and which need attention.
euharlee.treeAngle: creek-bottom hardwoods and pines by the covered bridge
